hinge: When primary incisors are intruded and touching developing permanent tooth buds, extraction is indicated to prevent damage (dilaceration/hypoplasia) to the permanent successors. Allowing spontaneous eruption risks further trauma to the developing tooth buds.
Approximately 74% of accessory canals are located in the apical third of the root. This is a standardized board fact regarding root canal anatomy and lateral/accessory canal distribution.

For a 7-year-old with 5 mm intrusion of a permanent incisor, allowing spontaneous re-eruption for up to 3 weeks is the standard first-line approach before considering surgical or orthodontic repositioning.

When VRF is suspected in an endodontically treated tooth with post and crown and an isolated deep pocket, CBCT is the recommended initial investigation as it provides 3D visualization of fracture lines that may not be visible on 2D radiographs.

Tooth Slooth is an instrument specifically designed to detect cracks and incomplete crown-root fractures by applying controlled selective pressure on individual cusps. D16 explorer and D1 explorer are for caries detection.

A tooth that has been retreated once already showing a distal crack line extending across the tooth surface with methylene blue dye and an isolated deep pocket is consistent with vertical root fracture. The prognosis for another retreatment is poor.

A gingival abscess associated with a primary molar indicates the pulp has become necrotic and the infection has tracked through the furcation or apex into the gingival tissues — irreversible pulpitis/necrosis. Hyperemia and reversible pulpitis do not cause abscess formation.

An ankylotic, high-pitched metallic sound on percussion is classic for intrusion injuries where the tooth is driven into the alveolar bone, creating a bone-to-tooth contact that produces this characteristic sound.
Avulsion causes pulp necrosis in nearly 100% of closed-apex teeth because the neurovascular bundle is completely severed at the apical foramen. Concussion and subluxation rarely cause pulp necrosis.

A filling material placed only in the coronal-middle portion of a tooth with an open apex after trauma describes apexogenesis — vital pulp therapy where the radicular pulp is preserved for continued root development, not obturation of the entire canal.

Multi-surface caries (3-site) indicates extensive destruction with reduced remaining tooth structure, making surgical extraction more likely than a simple extraction. Gingival swelling and percussion/pain are clinical signs of infection, not criteria for extraction type selection.

A bruxer who fractures an amalgam restoration generates excessive occlusal forces. A cast metal restoration (full-coverage onlay or crown) is preferred as it provides cuspal protection and superior strength for parafunctional loads compared to composite or ceramic.

Furcation grade IV in an upper molar involves all roots and is the most severe classification. Hemisection (splitting the tooth) is often the treatment when one root is salvageable. Root resection of a single root is for Grade III; odontoplasty is for Grade I. The question is ambiguous without specifying root involvement, but Grade IV typically warrants extraction or hemisection.

White spot lesions around orthodontic brackets are demineralized enamel. Fluoride gel application promotes remineralization more effectively in this scenario than fluoride varnish since it can be applied with trays over the affected areas. Emphasis on OHI is preventive but will not reminaralize existing lesions.

Enamel spindles are odontoblast processes that extend from the dentinoenamel junction into the enamel. Cutting through enamel spindles during tooth preparation can cause pain because they contain pulpal cellular elements. Enamel tufts and lamellae are hypomineralized areas that do not contain cellular processes.

Fiber posts are radiolucent on radiographs, while flowable composite is radiopaque. Therefore, the radiopacity differential between a flowable composite and the radiolucent fiber post appears as the composite being more radiopaque. The post appears less radiopaque relative to the composite.

A deep Class II restoration with good margins but with bone loss in the area indicates the restoration has violated the biologic width or the overhang has caused periodontal inflammation. The periodontium is primarily affected, not the pulp.

High-filler composite (hybrid/nanofilled) provides the best combination of esthetics, strength, and wear resistance for posterior restorations. Low-filler composites are weaker; amalgam is not esthetic; RMGIC has inferior strength and wear resistance.

Water spray during cavity preparation primarily functions to dissipate frictional heat generated by the bur, preventing thermal damage to the pulp and surrounding dentin. Secondary benefits include debris removal and improved visibility.

When a tooth has a short clinical crown or is equigingival, clamping the adjacent tooth and extending the rubber dam provides the best isolation while maintaining a seal. The described technique allows for adequate isolation of the short tooth.

Pain from an amalgam restoration opposing a gold crown is classic galvanic pain/shock caused by dissimilar metals in contact. The definitive management is to replace the amalgam restoration with a non-metallic or same-metal restoration to eliminate the galvanic couple.

Working on the buccal of quadrant 1 (upper right) and palatal of quadrant 2 (upper left) places both areas on the maxillary arch. The 9 o'clock position provides the best direct vision and access for maxillary right buccal and left palatal surfaces for a right-handed operator.

After RCT through a conservative MO access on an existing amalgam restoration, if the remaining tooth structure is adequate, the amalgam can serve as a core build-up, and the tooth can be crowned. This conserves the existing restoration and tooth structure.

Stock tray retention with irreversible hydrocolloid (alginate) relies on mechanical retention through perforations and rim locks in the tray. Chemical adhesion is not a feature of alginate impression materials.

For caries extending to the DEJ, resin-modified glass ionomer cement (RMGIC) is ideal as it provides chemical adhesion to both dentin and enamel, fluoride release, and better mechanical properties than conventional GIC. Composite would be placed over this as a final layer in a sandwich technique.
Titanium alloy (typically Ti-6Al-4V) contains aluminum (6%) and vanadium (4%) as its main alloying elements. The aluminum provides strength; vanadium provides corrosion resistance.

The item describes dysplasia/hypoplasia at incisor tips and molar occlusal surfaces — this location pattern is characteristic of chronological enamel hypoplasia from a short-term systemic insult during crown formation. Among the options, fluorosis is the best fit though both tetracycline and fluorosis are intrinsic stains affecting all surfaces.

A special tray for final impression must be rigid to prevent distortion during seating and removal, ensuring accurate transfer of intraoral details to the cast. Flexibility and strength are secondary considerations.
Gold is the most ductile and malleable of all metals. A single ounce of gold can be drawn into a wire over 80 km long or beaten into a sheet covering hundreds of square feet.
The enamel hatchet is a hand instrument designed with a single-beveled cutting edge used to refine and finish buccal and lingual proximal walls of cavity preparations in posterior teeth.
